section.title: Visualize quantum circuits, measurements, and states

questions.stem: What does this script print?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)

print(type(qc.draw()).__name__)
print(type(qc.draw("mpl")).__name__)

explanation.correct: With no `output` argument, `QuantumCircuit.draw()` defaults to the text renderer and returns a `TextDrawing` object (its `__str__` produces the ASCII art). `draw("mpl")` returns a full `matplotlib.figure.Figure` — the object you can `savefig()` or embed — so the two printed class names are `TextDrawing` and `Figure`.

distractors.A: Confuses the printable object with a bare string: `draw()` returns a `TextDrawing`, not `str`; only `str(qc.draw())` or `draw('latex_source')` yields a plain string.
distractors.C: The mpl renderer returns the whole `Figure`, not a single `Axes`; returning the Figure is what lets you save or further style the drawing.
distractors.D: Both halves are wrong: the default is `TextDrawing` (not `str`) and `mpl` returns a `Figure` (not an `AxesImage`, which is an image-plot artist).

evidence.A: option claims ('str', 'Figure'); executed: draw() -> TextDrawing, draw('mpl') -> Figure (isinstance Figure=True) -> match=False
evidence.B: option claims ('TextDrawing', 'Figure'); executed: draw() -> TextDrawing, draw('mpl') -> Figure (isinstance Figure=True) -> match=True
evidence.C: option claims ('TextDrawing', 'Axes'); executed: draw() -> TextDrawing, draw('mpl') -> Figure (isinstance Figure=True) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims ('str', 'AxesImage'); executed: draw() -> TextDrawing, draw('mpl') -> Figure (isinstance Figure=True) -> match=False

questions.stem: This 3-qubit circuit has operations only on qubits 0 and 2; qubit 1 is untouched. Which `draw()` call removes the empty qubit-1 wire from the rendering, so only the active qubits are shown?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.x(0)
qc.x(2)
# qubit 1 carries no operations

explanation.correct: `idle_wires=False` tells the drawer to omit any qubit (or clbit) wire that has no operations on it, so the qubit-1 line disappears and only qubits 0 and 2 are drawn. It works the same across the text, mpl, and latex renderers.

distractors.A: `idle_wires=True` is the default value — it keeps every wire, so the empty qubit-1 line is still drawn.
distractors.C: `plot_barriers` only toggles whether `barrier` instructions are shown; it has no effect on idle qubit wires.
distractors.D: `reverse_bits` only flips the top-to-bottom ordering of the wires; all three wires, including the idle one, are still rendered.

evidence.A: rendered OK; qubit-1 wire present=True; hides idle q1=False
evidence.B: rendered OK; qubit-1 wire present=False; hides idle q1=True
evidence.C: rendered OK; qubit-1 wire present=True; hides idle q1=False
evidence.D: rendered OK; qubit-1 wire present=True; hides idle q1=False

questions.stem: In the default text drawing of this circuit, qubit `q_0` is on the top wire. What does passing `reverse_bits=True` to `draw()` change?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.x(0)

print(qc.draw("text", reverse_bits=True))

options.text: The wires are reordered so `q_1` is drawn on top and `q_0` on the bottom.
The control and target roles of two-qubit gates such as CX are swapped.
Only the classical bits are relabeled; the qubit wire order is unchanged.
The endianness of the measurement counts produced by the circuit is reversed.

explanation.correct: `reverse_bits` is a rendering-only option: it flips the top-to-bottom order in which the qubit (and clbit) wires are drawn. By default Qiskit puts qubit 0 on top; with `reverse_bits=True` the highest-index qubit goes on top, so `q_1` moves above `q_0`. The circuit's operations and its results are untouched.

distractors.B: Reversing the drawing order never changes gate semantics — the control and target qubits of a CX keep their roles; only their vertical positions in the picture move.
distractors.C: It reorders the qubit wires themselves (and clbits too), not merely classical-bit labels.
distractors.D: `draw()` only produces a picture; it does not execute the circuit, so it cannot change any measurement counts or their endianness.

evidence.A: default drawing puts q0_top, reverse_bits=True puts q1_top -> wire order flips (q1 on top) = True
evidence.B: circuit operations are ['x'] both before and after drawing; reverse_bits does not alter gate control/target roles
evidence.C: num_clbits=0; the reordering moves qubit wires (q0_top -> q1_top), not classical-bit labels
evidence.D: qc.draw returns TextDrawing (a rendering), no execution occurs, so no counts/endianness change

questions.stem: You want a circuit in which an `X` on qubit 0 runs only when the earlier measurement stored a 1 in `c[0]`, and you want it to render with a classical-control (If) block when drawn. In Qiskit SDK 2.x, which line correctly adds that conditional operation?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(1, 1)
qc.h(0)
qc.measure(0, 0)
# ... add the conditional X here, then qc.draw("text")

explanation.correct: In Qiskit 2.x classical feedforward is expressed with the `if_test` context manager. Operations added inside the `with qc.if_test((clbit, value)):` block run only when the condition holds, and the drawer renders them inside a boxed `If-0 ... End-0` block attached to the classical wire.

distractors.B: `InstructionSet.c_if` was the v0.x/v1 way to attach a condition, but it was removed in Qiskit 2.0 — calling it now raises `AttributeError`, so nothing is added to the circuit.
distractors.C: `InstructionSet` (what `qc.x(0)` returns) has no assignable `condition` attribute; this raises `AttributeError` and builds no conditional.
distractors.D: `QuantumCircuit.x()` accepts no `condition` keyword; passing one raises a `TypeError`.

evidence.A: built OK; rendered circuit shows an If-block = True
evidence.B: raised AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'c_if'
evidence.C: raised AttributeError: 'InstructionSet' object has no attribute 'condition'
evidence.D: raised TypeError: QuantumCircuit.x() got an unexpected keyword argument 'condition'

questions.stem: Instead of the `qc.draw()` method, you want to use the standalone drawer function to render circuit `qc` as text. Which import and call is correct in Qiskit 2.x?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)

explanation.correct: `circuit_drawer` is the function form of `QuantumCircuit.draw()` and lives in `qiskit.visualization`. Called as `circuit_drawer(qc, output="text")` it returns the same `TextDrawing` the method would; the two APIs are equivalent.

distractors.B: The old `qiskit.tools` namespace was removed; importing the drawer from there raises `ModuleNotFoundError`.
distractors.C: `circuit_drawer` is not re-exported from the top-level `qiskit` package, so this import raises `ImportError`.
distractors.D: There is no `draw_circuit` function — the drawer is named `circuit_drawer`, so this import raises `ImportError`.

evidence.A: import + call OK; returned TextDrawing
evidence.B: raised ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'qiskit.tools'
evidence.C: raised ImportError: cannot import name 'circuit_drawer' from 'qiskit' (/Users/majl/GitHub/qiskit-developer-cer
evidence.D: raised ImportError: cannot import name 'draw_circuit' from 'qiskit.visualization' (/Users/majl/GitHub/qiskit-d

questions.stem: This cell is meant to produce a Matplotlib figure of the circuit but raises an error. What is the correct fix?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)

fig = qc.draw(output="matplotlib")   # raises VisualizationError

options.text: Use `qc.draw(output="mpl")` — `"matplotlib"` is not a valid output type.
Keep the string but add the flag: `qc.draw(output="matplotlib", interactive=True)`.
The string is correct; install `pylatexenc` to make the Matplotlib renderer work.

explanation.correct: The `output` argument selects the renderer by a short name. The Matplotlib renderer's name is `"mpl"`, not `"matplotlib"`. Any unrecognized value raises `VisualizationError`; the accepted values are `text`, `mpl`, `latex`, and `latex_source`.

distractors.B: `"figure"` is also not a recognized output type — the same `VisualizationError` is raised. The Matplotlib output is spelled `"mpl"`.
distractors.C: Adding a flag does not fix an invalid output name; the `VisualizationError` about the unrecognized `"matplotlib"` value is raised before any rendering happens.
distractors.D: `pylatexenc` is a dependency of the LaTeX renderer, not `mpl`. The failure here is purely the invalid output string, so installing it changes nothing.

evidence.A: draw(output='mpl') -> Figure; is Figure=True
evidence.B: raised VisualizationError: 'Invalid output type figure selected. The only valid choices are text, latex, latex_source
evidence.C: raised VisualizationError: 'Invalid output type matplotlib selected. The only valid choices are text, latex, latex_so
evidence.D: draw(output='matplotlib') raised VisualizationError: 'Invalid output type matplotlib selected. The only valid choices are text, latex -> D's premise is false

questions.stem: When drawing this circuit you want each qubit wire annotated at its left edge with its starting state |0⟩. Which `draw()` call adds those initial-state labels?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)

explanation.correct: The `initial_state` argument (default `False`) tells the drawer to prefix every qubit and clbit wire with its initial value, so each qubit line begins with a `|0>` label. It is accepted by all of the drawer's output backends.

distractors.A: `plot_barriers` only controls whether `barrier` instructions are drawn; it adds no initial-state labels.
distractors.B: There is no `with_initial_state` parameter; the argument is named `initial_state`, so this spelling raises a `TypeError`.
distractors.D: `vertical_compression` adjusts the vertical spacing of the text drawing; it does not add `|0>` labels.

evidence.A: rendered OK; initial-state label '|0>' present=False
evidence.B: raised TypeError: QuantumCircuit.draw() got an unexpected keyword argument 'with_initial_state'
evidence.C: rendered OK; initial-state label '|0>' present=True
evidence.D: rendered OK; initial-state label '|0>' present=False

questions.stem: Select the TWO calls that each return a `matplotlib.figure.Figure` for circuit `qc`. (Select 2.)
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.visualization import circuit_drawer

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)

explanation.correct: The Matplotlib renderer is selected by the output name `"mpl"`, and it is available both as the method `qc.draw("mpl")` and as the function `circuit_drawer(qc, output="mpl")`. Both return a `matplotlib.figure.Figure`.

distractors.C: `"matplotlib"` is not a valid output value — only `"mpl"` selects that renderer — so this raises `VisualizationError`.
distractors.D: With no output argument, `draw()` defaults to the text renderer and returns a `TextDrawing`, not a `Figure`.
distractors.E: `"figure"` is not a recognized output value; the Matplotlib output is `"mpl"`, so this raises `VisualizationError`.

evidence.A: returned Figure; is matplotlib Figure=True
evidence.B: returned Figure; is matplotlib Figure=True
evidence.C: raised VisualizationError: 'Invalid output type matplotlib selected. The only valid choices are text, latex, latex_so
evidence.D: returned TextDrawing; is matplotlib Figure=False
evidence.E: raised VisualizationError: 'Invalid output type figure selected. The only valid choices are text, latex, latex_source

questions.stem: You are preparing a figure of a quantum circuit for a published paper: you want colored gate boxes, a clean layout, and to save it as a high-resolution PNG with `savefig`. Which `draw()` output should you use?

options.text: `output="mpl"` — returns a Matplotlib `Figure` you can style and save with `savefig`.
`output="text"` — returns ASCII art, ideal for quick inspection in a terminal.
`output="latex_source"` — returns a raw LaTeX string rather than a rendered image.
the default `draw()` with no argument — gives you the same ASCII text drawing.

explanation.correct: The `mpl` renderer returns a `matplotlib.figure.Figure`, which supports colored gate boxes, style customization, and `fig.savefig(...)` to any raster or vector format. That is exactly what a publication-quality circuit figure needs.

distractors.B: The text renderer produces monochrome ASCII intended for terminals and logs; it cannot make colored boxes or a savable raster image.
distractors.C: `latex_source` returns a LaTeX string you would still have to compile yourself; it is not a ready-to-save figure and offers no Matplotlib styling.
distractors.D: With no argument `draw()` defaults to the text (ASCII) renderer — the opposite of the styleable, high-resolution figure you want.

questions.stem: This runs on a noiseless simulator. Which single bitstring carries probability 1 — i.e., where would `plot_histogram` place its one bar?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.x(0)
qc.x(2)
sv = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)

print(sv.probabilities_dict())

explanation.correct: Qiskit labels outcomes little-endian: the string is written q2 q1 q0 (highest-index qubit on the left). X flips qubits 0 and 2 to |1>, qubit 1 stays |0>, so the label is 1·0·1 = '101'. The single histogram bar sits at '101'.

distractors.A: '011' applies the reverse convention (q0 q1 q2 left-to-right). Qiskit puts qubit 0 on the RIGHT, so the set bits land at the rightmost and leftmost positions, giving '101'.
distractors.C: '110' also mis-orders the bits; the flipped qubits (0 and 2) map to the rightmost and leftmost characters, giving '101', not '110'.
distractors.D: `probabilities_dict` keys are binary bitstrings; '5' is the decimal index, which is not how the histogram labels are written.

evidence.A: option claims outcome '011'; executed probabilities_dict has peak np.str_('101') at p=1.0 (little-endian q2 q1 q0) -> match=False
evidence.B: option claims outcome '101'; executed probabilities_dict has peak np.str_('101') at p=1.0 (little-endian q2 q1 q0) -> match=True
evidence.C: option claims outcome '110'; executed probabilities_dict has peak np.str_('101') at p=1.0 (little-endian q2 q1 q0) -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims outcome '5'; executed probabilities_dict has peak np.str_('101') at p=1.0 (little-endian q2 q1 q0) -> match=False

questions.stem: You have a raw counts dictionary from 1024 shots. Compared with `plot_histogram(counts)`, what does `plot_distribution(counts)` display for the same data?

options.text: `plot_distribution` normalizes the bars into a distribution summing to 1, whereas `plot_histogram` shows the raw integer counts (summing to the shot total).
They are identical; `plot_distribution` is a deprecated alias of `plot_histogram`.
`plot_distribution` shows raw counts and `plot_histogram` normalizes to probabilities — the roles are reversed.
`plot_distribution` requires a `Statevector` rather than a counts dict, so it raises a `TypeError` here.

explanation.correct: `plot_histogram` plots the raw counts (its y-axis is labeled Count and the bar heights add up to the number of shots). `plot_distribution` plots a normalized (quasi-)probability distribution, so its bar heights add up to 1. Both accept the same counts dict.

distractors.B: They are separate functions with different y-axes; `plot_distribution` is neither an alias nor deprecated.
distractors.C: This reverses reality — `plot_histogram` shows raw counts and `plot_distribution` shows normalized probabilities, not the other way around.
distractors.D: `plot_distribution` accepts a counts / quasi-probability dict directly; it does not require a `Statevector`, so it does not raise here.

evidence.A: plot_histogram heights sum=1024.0 (shots=1024, ylabel='Count'); plot_distribution heights sum=1.0 (ylabel='Quasi-probability') -> hist=raw counts, dist=normalized: True
evidence.B: the two differ: histogram sum=1024.0 vs distribution sum=1.0, ylabels 'Count' vs 'Quasi-probability' -> not an alias
evidence.C: observed histogram is raw (sum=1024.0) and distribution is normalized (sum=1.0); the reversed claim is false
evidence.D: plot_distribution(counts_dict) succeeded=True (returned a Figure); it does not require a Statevector

questions.stem: The counts dict has four distinct bitstrings. What does `plot_histogram(counts, number_to_keep=2)` put on the x-axis?

options.text: The two highest-count bitstrings ('00' and '11') plus one aggregated bar labeled 'rest'.
Only the two highest-count bitstrings ('00' and '11'); the other outcomes are dropped entirely.
The first two bitstrings in insertion order ('01' and '00'), with no aggregation.
All four bitstrings; `number_to_keep` only changes the figure width.

explanation.correct: `number_to_keep=N` keeps the N bitstrings with the largest counts and folds every remaining outcome into a single bar labeled 'rest', so no probability weight is lost. Here the two largest are '00' and '11' (500 each), giving bars ['00', '11', 'rest'].

distractors.B: The remaining outcomes are not discarded — their combined weight is shown as the 'rest' bar, so the bars still account for all shots.
distractors.C: Selection is by count magnitude (the largest bars), not by dictionary insertion order, and the remainder IS aggregated into 'rest'.
distractors.D: `number_to_keep` controls how many individual bars are shown, not the figure size.

evidence.A: x-axis labels = ['00', '11', 'rest']; top-2 counts kept + 'rest' bin, 3 bars total = True
evidence.B: labels=['00', '11', 'rest']; a 'rest' bar is present (True) so remaining outcomes are NOT dropped
evidence.C: labels=['00', '11', 'rest']; kept bars are the highest-count '00','11' not insertion-first '01','00'; aggregation present=True
evidence.D: labels=['00', '11', 'rest']; only 3 bars shown (not all four=False) -> number_to_keep changes bars, not width

questions.stem: After running a `SamplerV2` job, this code tries to plot the measurement results but raises `AttributeError`. Which change plots the histogram correctly?
questions.code:
from qiskit.visualization import plot_histogram

# result = SamplerV2(mode=backend).run([isa_qc]).result()
data = result[0].data.meas      # a BitArray
fig = plot_histogram(data)      # AttributeError

options.text: Keep the BitArray but pass shots: `plot_histogram(data, shots=1024)`

explanation.correct: `plot_histogram` expects a counts mapping (bitstring -> integer). A `SamplerV2` result stores per-shot data as a `BitArray`; call its `.get_counts()` to obtain the counts dict, then pass that. `result[0].data.meas.get_counts()` is the fix.

distractors.A: `result[0].data` is a `DataBin` container; you still have to reach into the register's `BitArray` and call `get_counts()` before plotting.
distractors.B: `result[0]` is a `PubResult`, not a counts mapping; `plot_histogram` cannot read counts from it and raises `AttributeError`.
distractors.D: A `BitArray` has no counts-mapping interface, and `plot_histogram` has no `shots` keyword — this raises `TypeError`. You must call `get_counts()` first.

evidence.A: raised TypeError: int() argument must be a string, a bytes-like object or a real number, not 'BitArray'
evidence.B: raised AttributeError: 'SamplerPubResult' object has no attribute 'values'
evidence.C: call returned Figure; is Figure=True
evidence.D: raised TypeError: plot_histogram() got an unexpected keyword argument 'shots'

questions.stem: You ran the same circuit before and after error mitigation and want one histogram that overlays the two count dictionaries side by side, with a labeled legend. Which call does this?

explanation.correct: To overlay several result sets, pass a LIST of counts dicts as the single data argument and give a matching `legend` list. `plot_histogram([before, after], legend=[...])` draws grouped bars per bitstring with the two runs color-coded and labeled.

distractors.A: Two dicts cannot be combined with `+` (that raises `TypeError`); overlaying requires a list of dicts, not addition.
distractors.B: `plot_histogram` takes one data argument; a second positional dict is consumed by the next parameter (`figsize`), which raises an error instead of overlaying.
distractors.C: A dict-of-dicts is not a list of counts; `plot_histogram` tries to read the outer dict as counts and raises a `TypeError`.

evidence.A: raised TypeError: unsupported operand type(s) for +: 'dict' and 'dict'
evidence.B: raised VisualizationError: "Length of legend (2) doesn't match number of input executions (1)."
evidence.C: raised TypeError: int() argument must be a string, a bytes-like object or a real number, not 'dict'
evidence.D: returned Figure; legend=['before', 'after']; overlays both runs=True

questions.stem: Noiseless simulation. What outcome probabilities does this print — i.e., which bar dominates the histogram?
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.ry(2 * np.pi / 3, 0)
sv = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)

print({k: round(v, 3) for k, v in sv.probabilities_dict().items()})

options.text: `{'0': 0.25, '1': 0.75}` — the '1' bar is three times taller.

explanation.correct: RY(θ)|0> = cos(θ/2)|0> + sin(θ/2)|1>, so P(1) = sin²(θ/2). With θ = 2π/3, θ/2 = π/3 and sin²(π/3) = 3/4. Thus P(0) = 0.25 and P(1) = 0.75, and the histogram's '1' bar is three times the height of the '0' bar.

distractors.A: This uses the full angle (cos²θ / sin²θ) instead of the half-angle θ/2. RY's rotation is by θ/2 on the state, giving the 0.25 / 0.75 split, not 0.933 / 0.067.
distractors.B: This swaps the two probabilities. P(1) = sin²(θ/2) = 0.75 is the larger one, so '1' dominates, not '0'.
distractors.C: Equal bars would need θ = π/2 (a balanced superposition); at θ = 2π/3 the state is biased toward |1>.

evidence.A: option claims P(0)=0.933, P(1)=0.067; executed P(0)=0.25, P(1)=0.75 -> match=False
evidence.B: option claims P(0)=0.75, P(1)=0.25; executed P(0)=0.25, P(1)=0.75 -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims P(0)=0.5, P(1)=0.5; executed P(0)=0.25, P(1)=0.75 -> match=False
evidence.D: option claims P(0)=0.25, P(1)=0.75; executed P(0)=0.25, P(1)=0.75 -> match=True

questions.stem: The counts dictionary below is built in a scrambled order. With no extra arguments, how does `plot_histogram(counts)` arrange the bars, and what does it return?

options.text: In the dictionary's insertion order ('11','00','10','01'); returns a matplotlib `Figure`.
Sorted by count with the tallest bar first; returns a matplotlib `Figure`.
Sorted in ascending bitstring order ('00','01','10','11'); returns a matplotlib `Figure`.
Sorted ascending, but the call returns an `Axes` object rather than a `Figure`.

explanation.correct: By default `plot_histogram` sorts the x-axis labels in ascending lexicographic (bitstring) order, independent of insertion order or bar height, and returns a `matplotlib.figure.Figure`. So the bars appear as '00','01','10','11'.

distractors.A: The bars are not left in insertion order; the default `sort='asc'` reorders them by bitstring value.
distractors.B: Sorting by count is not the default — you would need `sort='value_desc'`; by default it sorts by the bitstring label.
distractors.D: `plot_histogram` returns the whole `Figure`, not a bare `Axes`; returning the Figure is what lets you save or restyle it.

evidence.A: observed labels ['00', '01', '10', '11'], Figure=True; insertion order ['11', '00', '10', '01'] -> match=False
evidence.B: observed labels ['00', '01', '10', '11']; count-descending order ['00', '11', '01', '10'] -> match=False
evidence.C: observed labels ['00', '01', '10', '11'], returns matplotlib Figure=True; ascending order ['00', '01', '10', '11'] -> match=True
evidence.D: observed labels ['00', '01', '10', '11']; return type is Figure (Figure=True) -> Axes-not-Figure match=False

questions.stem: You call `plot_bloch_multivector(state)` on this 3-qubit statevector. How many Bloch spheres are drawn, and what does the call return?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector
from qiskit.visualization import plot_bloch_multivector

qc = QuantumCircuit(3)
qc.h(0)
qc.x(1)
state = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)
fig = plot_bloch_multivector(state)

options.text: A single Bloch sphere summarizing the whole 3-qubit state; returns a `Figure`.
One Bloch sphere per qubit (3 spheres); returns a matplotlib `Figure`.
2^3 = 8 Bloch spheres, one per computational basis state; returns a `Figure`.
One sphere per qubit, but the call returns a list of `Axes` instead of a `Figure`.

explanation.correct: `plot_bloch_multivector` computes each qubit's reduced (single-qubit) state and draws one Bloch sphere per qubit, packed into a single `matplotlib.figure.Figure`. A 3-qubit state therefore yields 3 spheres.

distractors.A: It does not collapse the whole register into one sphere — a single sphere can only represent one qubit's reduced state.
distractors.C: The number of spheres tracks the number of qubits (n), not the number of basis states (2^n).
distractors.D: The function returns the whole `Figure`; the per-qubit axes live inside it.

evidence.A: drew 3 Bloch sphere(s), Figure=True; single-sphere claim match=False
evidence.B: drew 3 spheres for 3 qubits, returns matplotlib Figure=True; one-per-qubit match=True
evidence.C: drew 3 spheres; 2^3=8 claim match=False
evidence.D: return type is Figure (Figure=True); list-of-Axes claim match=False

questions.stem: After this circuit, `plot_bloch_multivector` draws the single qubit's arrow along which axis, and which state does that arrow represent?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit

qc = QuantumCircuit(1)
qc.h(0)
qc.s(0)

explanation.correct: H sends |0> to |+> (Bloch +X). The S gate = diag(1, i) adds a phase i to the |1> amplitude, rotating the vector 90° about Z from +X to +Y. The resulting state (|0> + i|1>)/√2 has Bloch vector (⟨X⟩,⟨Y⟩,⟨Z⟩) = (0, 1, 0), so the arrow points along +Y.

distractors.A: +X is the state right after H, before S. Applying S rotates the vector off the X axis by 90° about Z.
distractors.B: −Z (state |1>) would require flipping the qubit; H followed by S keeps it on the equator, not at a pole.
distractors.D: +Z (state |0>) is the starting point before any gate; H already moved the vector to the equator.

evidence.A: option claims axis +X -> Bloch (1.0, 0.0, 0.0); executed state Bloch vector = (0.0, 1.0, 0.0) -> match=False
evidence.B: option claims axis -Z -> Bloch (0.0, 0.0, -1.0); executed state Bloch vector = (0.0, 1.0, 0.0) -> match=False
evidence.C: option claims axis +Y -> Bloch (0.0, 1.0, 0.0); executed state Bloch vector = (0.0, 1.0, 0.0) -> match=True
evidence.D: option claims axis +Z -> Bloch (0.0, 0.0, 1.0); executed state Bloch vector = (0.0, 1.0, 0.0) -> match=False

questions.stem: On a single-qubit Bloch sphere the arrow points along the −X axis, i.e. the Bloch vector is (−1, 0, 0). Which state does this visualization represent?

explanation.correct: The Bloch vector is (⟨X⟩,⟨Y⟩,⟨Z⟩). A vector on the −X axis, (−1,0,0), means ⟨X⟩ = −1: the state is the −1 eigenstate of X, which is |−> = (|0> − |1>)/√2.

distractors.A: |0> sits at the north pole, Bloch vector (0,0,+1), not on the −X axis.
distractors.C: |1> sits at the south pole, Bloch vector (0,0,−1).
distractors.D: |+> is the +1 eigenstate of X, Bloch vector (+1,0,0) — the opposite end of the X axis from the arrow shown.

evidence.A: option state has Bloch vector (0.0, 0.0, 1.0); target arrow is (-1.0, 0.0, 0.0) (-X axis) -> match=False
evidence.B: option state has Bloch vector (-1.0, 0.0, 0.0); target arrow is (-1.0, 0.0, 0.0) (-X axis) -> match=True
evidence.C: option state has Bloch vector (0.0, 0.0, -1.0); target arrow is (-1.0, 0.0, 0.0) (-X axis) -> match=False
evidence.D: option state has Bloch vector (1.0, 0.0, 0.0); target arrow is (-1.0, 0.0, 0.0) (-X axis) -> match=False

questions.stem: You have a `Statevector` `sv` and want to render it as a q-sphere figure directly from the object. Which call does that?
questions.code:
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

sv = Statevector.from_label("01")

explanation.correct: `Statevector.draw(output=...)` is the object's entry point to the state-visualization drawers. Passing `"qsphere"` returns the q-sphere `Figure` (equivalent to `plot_state_qsphere(sv)`). Other valid tokens include `"bloch"`, `"city"`, `"hinton"`, and `"paulivec"`.

distractors.A: `Statevector` has no `qsphere()` method; state drawing goes through `draw(output=...)`, so this raises `AttributeError`.
distractors.B: There is no top-level `plot_qsphere`; the function is named `plot_state_qsphere`, so this import/name fails.
distractors.D: `"q_sphere"` (with an underscore) is not a recognized output token; the correct spelling is `"qsphere"`, so this raises a `ValueError`.

evidence.A: raised AttributeError: 'Statevector' object has no attribute 'qsphere'
evidence.B: raised ImportError: cannot import name 'plot_qsphere' from 'qiskit.visualization' (/Users/majl/GitHu
evidence.C: sv.draw('qsphere') -> Figure; Figure=True
evidence.D:
raised ValueError: 'q_sphere' is not a valid option for drawing Statevector
             objects. P

questions.stem: You prepare the Bell state (|00> + |11>)/√2 and call `plot_bloch_multivector(state)`. What do the two Bloch spheres show?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
state = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)

options.text: Both arrows point to the north pole — each qubit is in |0>.
Both arrows point along +X — each qubit is in |+>.
The two spheres are joined by a single shared arrow representing the entanglement.
Each arrow shrinks to the center (zero length): each qubit's reduced state is maximally mixed.

explanation.correct: `plot_bloch_multivector` draws each qubit's reduced (single-qubit) state, obtained by tracing out the other qubit. For a Bell pair each reduced state is the maximally mixed I/2, whose Bloch vector is (0,0,0). So both arrows collapse to the center — the visual signature of maximal entanglement.

distractors.A: North pole (0,0,+1) would mean each qubit is separately in |0>, but entangling with the CX destroys any definite single-qubit state.
distractors.B: |+> is a pure equatorial state (length-1 vector); the reduced state of a Bell pair is not pure, so the vector is not on the surface.
distractors.C: The multivector plot renders each qubit's reduced state independently — it cannot draw a shared arrow; entanglement instead appears as zero-length vectors.

evidence.A: reduced Bloch vectors b0=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0), b1=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0); north-pole (0,0,1) claim match=False
evidence.C: each qubit is rendered from its reduced state (b0=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0), b1=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0), length 0.0); no shared/entangled arrow is drawable
evidence.D: reduced states maximally mixed: b0=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0), b1=(0.0, 0.0, 0.0) (length 0.0), purity=0.5 -> arrows at origin, match=True

questions.stem: On a q-sphere, a node (marker) is drawn at every computational basis state whose amplitude is nonzero. For the state below, which basis states appear as nodes? (Select 2.)
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector
from qiskit.visualization import plot_state_qsphere

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.x(1)
state = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)
fig = plot_state_qsphere(state)

explanation.correct: H puts qubit 0 into a superposition and X sets qubit 1 to |1>. In Qiskit's little-endian labels (q1 q0) the state is (|10> + |11>)/√2, so only |10> and |11> have nonzero amplitude and appear as q-sphere nodes. Node size encodes each amplitude's magnitude and node color its phase.

distractors.A: |00> requires qubit 1 to be |0>, but the X gate flipped qubit 1 to |1>, so |00> has zero amplitude and no node.
distractors.B: |01> also needs qubit 1 in |0>; since qubit 1 is |1>, this amplitude is zero and no node is drawn.

evidence.A: basis |00>: |amplitude|=0 -> node present=False
evidence.B: basis |01>: |amplitude|=0 -> node present=False
evidence.C: basis |10>: |amplitude|=0.7071 -> node present=True
evidence.D: basis |11>: |amplitude|=0.7071 -> node present=True

questions.stem: On the q-sphere of this state, the node for |01> is drawn in a different color from the node for |10>, and the two nodes are the same size. What does that color difference encode?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector
from qiskit.visualization import plot_state_qsphere

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
qc.x(0)
qc.z(0)
state = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)
fig = plot_state_qsphere(state)

options.text: The amplitude magnitude — the |01> node stands for a larger amplitude than |10>.
The relative phase of the amplitude — the |01> branch carries phase π while |10> has phase 0.
The Hamming weight (number of 1-bits) of the basis state.
Nothing meaningful — q-sphere nodes are all the same color and any difference is a rendering artifact.

explanation.correct: On a q-sphere a node's SIZE encodes the amplitude magnitude and its COLOR encodes the amplitude's complex phase. Here |01> and |10> have equal magnitude (same size) but the Z gate multiplies the |01> branch by −1, giving it phase π versus phase 0 for |10> — so the colors differ.

distractors.A: The two amplitudes have equal magnitude (the nodes are the same size); magnitude is shown by size, not color.
distractors.C: |01> and |10> have the same Hamming weight (one 1-bit each), so a color difference cannot be encoding Hamming weight — it is the phase that differs.
distractors.D: q-sphere nodes ARE color-coded by phase; the difference is meaningful, not an artifact.

evidence.A: |amp(01)|=0.7071, |amp(10)|=0.7071; magnitudes equal=True; '01 larger magnitude' match=False
evidence.B: phase(01)=3.1416 rad vs phase(10)=0.0 rad (differ by pi=True); magnitudes equal=True -> color encodes relative phase, match=True
evidence.C: Hamming weights 01->1, 10->1 (equal); a color difference cannot encode Hamming weight, match=False
evidence.D: amplitudes differ in phase (3.1416 vs 0.0); nodes are not identical, so not an artifact, match=False

questions.stem: What input does each of `plot_bloch_vector` and `plot_bloch_multivector` expect?

options.text: Both take a quantum state; they differ only in the resulting figure size.
`plot_bloch_vector` takes a quantum state; `plot_bloch_multivector` takes a 3-number vector [x, y, z].
`plot_bloch_vector` takes a 3-number Bloch vector [x, y, z]; `plot_bloch_multivector` takes a quantum state and draws one sphere per qubit.
Both take a 3-number vector [x, y, z]; `plot_bloch_multivector` just repeats it on several spheres.

explanation.correct: `plot_bloch_vector` draws a single sphere from an explicit Bloch vector [x, y, z] (Cartesian by default). `plot_bloch_multivector` takes a quantum state (Statevector or DensityMatrix), computes each qubit's reduced state, and draws one Bloch sphere per qubit. Their inputs are different kinds of object.

distractors.A: `plot_bloch_vector` does not accept a quantum state — it expects the three numbers of a Bloch vector — so the two functions differ in input type, not just size.
distractors.B: This reverses the two: it is `plot_bloch_vector` that takes the 3-number vector and `plot_bloch_multivector` that takes a state.
distractors.D: `plot_bloch_multivector` rejects a bare 3-number vector (it raises 'Input is not a multi-qubit quantum state'); it needs an actual state.

evidence.A: plot_bloch_vector accepts a state=False, plot_bloch_multivector accepts a state=True; 'both take a state' match=False
evidence.B: plot_bloch_vector accepts a state=False, plot_bloch_multivector accepts a 3-vector=False; reversed claim match=False
evidence.C: plot_bloch_vector accepts [x,y,z]=True; plot_bloch_multivector accepts a state=True and rejects a 3-vector=True; match=True
evidence.D: plot_bloch_vector accepts [x,y,z]=True, plot_bloch_multivector accepts [x,y,z]=False; 'both take a 3-vector' match=False

questions.stem: You have the 2-qubit Bell state and want one Bloch sphere per qubit, but this line does not do that — `plot_bloch_vector` treats its argument as a raw [x, y, z] vector. Which call draws one sphere per qubit?
questions.code:
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
from qiskit.quantum_info import Statevector
from qiskit.visualization import plot_bloch_vector, plot_bloch_multivector

qc = QuantumCircuit(2)
qc.h(0)
qc.cx(0, 1)
state = Statevector.from_instruction(qc)
fig = plot_bloch_vector(state)   # does NOT produce per-qubit spheres

options.text: `plot_bloch_vector(state, coord_type="spherical")` — reinterpret the same input as spherical coordinates.
`plot_bloch_vector(state.data)` — pass the raw amplitude array instead.
`plot_bloch_multivector([0, 0, 1])` — hand a Bloch vector to the multivector function.
`plot_bloch_multivector(state)` — pass the quantum state to the multivector drawer.

explanation.correct: `plot_bloch_multivector` is the function that takes a quantum state and renders one Bloch sphere per qubit (each from that qubit's reduced state). For the 2-qubit Bell state it produces two spheres. `plot_bloch_vector`, by contrast, only ever draws a single sphere from a 3-number vector.

distractors.A: `coord_type` only changes how the three numbers of a vector are interpreted (Cartesian vs spherical); it still cannot take a quantum state — the length-4 state fails to unpack into three coordinates and raises.
distractors.B: `state.data` is the length-4 amplitude array, not a Bloch vector; `plot_bloch_vector` still expects exactly [x, y, z] and draws one meaningless sphere, not one per qubit.
distractors.C: `plot_bloch_multivector` expects a quantum STATE, not a 3-number vector; passing [0, 0, 1] raises 'Input is not a multi-qubit quantum state'.

evidence.A: result: raised ValueError Bloch-sphere axes (need 2, one per qubit) -> correct=False
evidence.B: result: 1 Bloch-sphere axes (need 2, one per qubit) -> correct=False
evidence.C: result: raised VisualizationError Bloch-sphere axes (need 2, one per qubit) -> correct=False
evidence.D: result: 2 Bloch-sphere axes (need 2, one per qubit) -> correct=True

questions.stem: Each snippet is applied to a fresh single-qubit circuit `qc = QuantumCircuit(1)`. For which TWO does `plot_bloch_multivector` place the arrow exactly on the equator of the Bloch sphere (⟨Z⟩ = 0)? (Select 2.)
questions.code:
import numpy as np
from qiskit import QuantumCircuit
# each option acts on a fresh:  qc = QuantumCircuit(1)

explanation.correct: A point is on the equator when ⟨Z⟩ = 0. `h(0)` makes |+> with Bloch (1,0,0), and `h(0); s(0)` makes (|0>+i|1>)/√2 with Bloch (0,1,0) — both have ⟨Z⟩ = 0, so their arrows lie on the equator.

distractors.B: `x(0)` makes |1>, Bloch (0,0,−1) — the south pole, ⟨Z⟩ = −1, not the equator.
distractors.C: `ry(π/3, 0)` gives ⟨Z⟩ = cos(π/3) = 0.5, so the arrow sits in the northern hemisphere, off the equator.
distractors.E: An empty circuit leaves the qubit in |0>, Bloch (0,0,+1) — the north pole, ⟨Z⟩ = +1.

evidence.A: <Z> = 0.0 -> on equator (|<Z>|~0) = True
evidence.B: <Z> = -1.0 -> on equator (|<Z>|~0) = False
evidence.C: <Z> = 0.5 -> on equator (|<Z>|~0) = False
evidence.D: <Z> = 0.0 -> on equator (|<Z>|~0) = True
